title: Why No One Can Predict the Future of AI
description: Since the economics of AI are so distorted by subsidies, what happens when we actually have to watch our token usage?
contentMarkdown:
A lot of people talk about AI as if the hard part is predicting model capability. Will models get smarter? Will they reason better? Will they code more autonomously? Will they become agents?

Those questions matter, but I think the harder thing to predict right now is the economics.

What will people consistently pay for? What kind of usage is actually durable? Which product behaviors are real, and which ones are being shaped by a temporary moment of aggressive subsidies, competition, and unusually cheap access?

That is the part I do not think anyone can answer with much confidence yet.

The problem is not that AI is fake. It is not. The problem is that people are trying to make long-term economic predictions from what is probably a short-term and distorted phase.

We may be mistaking subsidized behavior for durable demand.

That is the core issue.

People are using AI constantly right now. They are chatting with it, coding with it, asking it to summarize things, research things, write things, plan things, and increasingly act on their behalf. Companies are building products around large context windows, generous quotas, fast responses, and workflows that assume constant model access. Founders are taking current usage as evidence of future product categories. Investors are taking adoption curves as evidence of future market size. Users are getting trained on a certain standard of abundance.

But how much of that behavior survives once the economics harden?

That is a different question.

It is entirely possible that current usage patterns are being shaped by an environment that is not economically normal. AI labs and AI products are competing hard, subsidizing access in various ways, and trying to build habit before the market settles. That can be a rational strategy. It also makes the signals noisier. If a user adopts a behavior under unusually favorable pricing, low friction, and high model generosity, that does not automatically tell you what they will do later under tighter limits, worse unit economics, stricter quotas, or a more disciplined willingness-to-pay environment.

A lot of people are treating current usage as clean evidence of future demand. I do not think it is that clean.

This matters because token economics are not some backend implementation detail. They shape the whole system.

They shape product design. They shape which features feel viable. They shape how often users engage. They shape whether a product can be habit-forming or only used occasionally. They shape whether a company can afford to be generous, whether a workflow can stay always-on, and whether an experience feels magical or constrained.

If inference gets dramatically cheaper, a lot of current product dreams start looking much more realistic. Fully personal, always-on agents become easier to imagine. Products can keep more context, act more frequently, and stay embedded in daily life without constantly fighting the math.

If inference does not get cheap enough, or if pricing tightens in ways the market does not like, behavior changes. Products get narrower. Usage becomes more selective. Some current expectations turn out to have been built on a temporary cost structure rather than a durable one.

That is why I think a lot of AI forecasting is really pricing forecasting in disguise.

People say they are predicting the future of software, work, consumer behavior, or agents. A lot of the time they are implicitly making a bet on token costs, margins, and what kind of usage can be sustained once the current market gets less forgiving.

And to be clear, this does not mean the technology is not real.

That is where I think some people get too binary. If the economics are distorted, they jump to the conclusion that the technology must be overhyped. That does not follow.

The internet is the obvious example. There was enormous overbuilding, bad business models, speculative mania, and a crash. Capital outran economic clarity. Plenty of companies died. Plenty of narratives turned out to be nonsense. And yet the internet still became one of the most important technologies in modern life.

The bubble did not mean the internet was fake. It meant people were early, messy, overconfident, and often wrong about where the value would settle.

Railroads looked like that too. Telecom and fiber had their own versions of this. General-purpose technologies often attract too much capital before they attract the right economic understanding. The underlying capability can be transformative even while the surrounding market is noisy, overbuilt, and temporarily irrational.

I think AI is probably like that.

It can be both true that AI is going to matter a lot and true that we still do not understand the steady-state economics very well. Those views are not in conflict. In fact, they probably belong together.

The strongest position right now is not blind optimism or reflexive skepticism. It is something more like this: AI is likely to be deeply important, but many of the current economic signals are being generated inside a strange and probably temporary environment.

That should make us more cautious about confident predictions.

It should make founders more careful about what usage patterns they treat as durable. It should make investors more careful about what adoption curves really mean. It should make users more aware that today’s product experience may not map cleanly onto tomorrow’s pricing structure. And it should make anyone talking about “the future of AI” a little more humble about how much of that future depends not just on intelligence, but on cost.

Because before we reach the era of fully personal and always-on agents, which I am looking forward to, we need to get through the messier phase where the economics stop being so distorted and start revealing what people actually value enough to keep paying for.

That is the part no one can predict cleanly yet.

Not because AI is trivial. Not because nothing useful is happening. But because we are still trying to infer long-term truths from a market that probably has not reached anything close to equilibrium.

The question is not whether AI matters.

It is what survives once the subsidies fade, the pricing settles, and the market has to stand on firmer ground.
